answer:
In classical Urdu *ghazal* poetry, as represented in this corpus, *faqir* (فقیر) denotes a mendicant or wandering beggar-ascetic, typically a Sufi figure who outwardly renounces worldly attachments and lives on alms (*gadai*). It often carries a layered connotation: an ideal of spiritual poverty and detachment from material comfort, but subject to ironic critique when the *faqir* hypocritically clings to ease (*rahat-parast*) rather than embracing true ascetic rigor (*riyazat*).[^1]

This distinction appears vividly in Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq's ghazal:

> درویش ہے وہی جو ریاضت میں چست ہو  
> تارک نہیں فقیر بھی راحت_پرست ہے  
> 
> *Darvesh hai wohi jo riyazat mein chust ho*  
> *Taarik nahin faqir bhi rahat-parast hai*[^2]

Rendered in English translation:

> The true Dervish is one who strives in sacred chore;  
> The mendicant renounces naught; a comfort-devotee is.[^3]

Here, the *darvesh* (درویش; dervish) embodies authentic ascetic discipline, while the *faqir* is dismissed as superficial—a false renouncer (*taarik nahin*) more devoted to personal comfort than spiritual trial. This trope reflects broader Sufi poetic irony, questioning outward piety versus inner truth.[^4]

Mirza Ghalib employs *faqir* similarly in a performative sense, as a guise for lovers mimicking beggars to evoke generosity from the beloved:

> بنا کر فقیروں کا ہم بھۃ غالب  
> تماشہ_اہل_کرم دیکھتے ہیں  
> 
> *Bana kar faqeeron ka hum bhes Ghalib*  
> *Tamasha-e-ahl-e-karam dekhte hain*

> Dressed in the guise of mendicants, O Ghalib, now  
> The grand performance of the generous we see.[^5]

No extended philosophical treatise on the *faqir* appears in the corpus's Urdu poetry holdings (primarily *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, *Diwan-e-Zauq*, and Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara*). The term aligns with its standard Sufi usage but is invoked poetically to probe authenticity, hypocrisy, and divine love over mere worldly abnegation.

[^1]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 25 (/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/25-kab-haq-parast-zahid-e-jannat-parast-hai/translation/).
[^2]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 25 (/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/25-kab-haq-parast-zahid-e-jannat-parast-hai/original/#p-d90e95).
[^3]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 25 (/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/25-kab-haq-parast-zahid-e-jannat-parast-hai/translation/#p-e44bc0).
[^4]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 25 (/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/25-kab-haq-parast-zahid-e-jannat-parast-hai/transliteration/#p-d90e95).
[^5]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 90 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/90-jahan-tera-naqsh-e-qadam-dekhte-hain/translation/#p-6b3ee1).
